metadata.title: Claude, By the Numbers
metadata.subtitle: The Data Plugin
metadata.audience: Practitioners and makers following the channel's Claude workflows
metadata.one_idea: Without the data plugin your numbers sit in spreadsheets you never open; with it Claude does data exploration and the unglamorous-but-essential data cleaning, then answers real questions (which clients are declining?) — but the analysis is only as trustworthy as the data and the assumptions, so you still own the judgment.

props.command: I have a year of revenue in a spreadsheet I never open. What's actually in it?
output: Three clients carry most of it.
One has been sliding since September.

beats.narration_text: Olá — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. Somewhere on your drive is a spreadsheet you're a little afraid of. The data plugin opens it, cleans it, and answers the question you've been avoiding — in plain English. Here's how far that goes, and where you still hold the pen.
beats.show: Claude composer on the cream stage: the avoided question typed in, the plain-English answer resolving below it.

props.command: I have a year of revenue in a spreadsheet I never open. What's actually in it?
output: Three clients carry most of it.
One has been sliding since September.

beats.narration_text: Start with the file you never open.
beats.show: Segment card: 'The Unopened Spreadsheet' in Title Case serif, warm ink on cream.

props.title: The Unopened Spreadsheet
props.sub: Start with the file you never open.

beats.narration_text: Here's what solo operators won't admit: they're afraid of their own data. A folder called Finances, twelve months of revenue inside it, never opened — because opening it means analysis, and analysis means formulas, and formulas mean an hour of Googling VLOOKUP syntax and still not trusting the answer.
beats.show: A folder icon labelled 'Finances' sits closed and dusty; a thought-bubble chain lights in turn — analysis, formulas, doubt.

props.label: Afraid of the numbers.
props.caption: Here's what solo operators won't admit: they're afraid of their own data. A folder called Finances, twelve months of rev

beats.narration_text: So without the plugin, understanding your numbers looks like this loop. Export a CSV from your payment processor. Open it. Stare at rows and columns. Get a vague sense things are up or down. Close the file. Go back to work.
beats.show: A four-step ring animates and closes on itself: export → stare → vague sense → close, then repeats.

beats.narration_text: And so the questions that actually matter go unanswered — not because they're unanswerable, but because answering them felt too hard. Decisions that should ride on data get made on gut feel instead.
beats.show: A still of a person at a desk, spreadsheet glow on their face, hand closing a laptop; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: solo operator at a dim desk closing a laptop mid-spreadsheet; documentary duotone, no readable numbers.

beats.narration_text: Now change how you ask.
beats.show: Segment card: 'Ask It Plainly' in Title Case serif on cream.

beats.narration_text: With the plugin, you point Claude at that same file and ask the way you'd ask a colleague: which clients bring in the most revenue — and has the mix changed over the last two quarters?
beats.show: A CSV flows in from the left; a plain-language question flows in beside it; a single sentence-answer resolves on the right.

beats.narration_text: No formula. No pivot table. Claude reads the data, runs the analysis, and hands back a sentence you can act on — in less time than it would take to write a SUM.
beats.show: Onda code-block: a composer line typing the plain-language query, then the answer rendered as a sentence, not a grid.

props.code:
> which clients generated the most revenue,
  and has the mix shifted over the past two quarters?

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
> which clients generated the most revenue,
  and has the mix shifted over the past two quarters?

beats.narration_text: That's natural-language querying. What were my total sales by month? Which client led last quarter? Am I spending more on software than I was six months ago? Claude translates each question into the right analysis, and returns a sentence — not a table of raw numbers.
beats.show: A spoken question funnels through a translation node into an analysis, then narrows to a single highlighted sentence.

beats.narration_text: It turns 'I think business is going okay' into something specific — say, three clients are sixty percent of revenue, and one's been sliding since September. That's the gap between reacting to problems and seeing them coming.
beats.show: Spark beat: a vague phrase dissolves and a specific, dated finding sets in its place; terracotta underline on 'seeing them coming'.

props.title: See it coming.
props.sub: It turns 'I think business is going okay' into something specific — say, three clients…

beats.narration_text: Beyond answering, four kinds of work.
beats.show: Segment card: 'What It Actually Does' in Title Case serif on cream.

props.title: What It Actually Does
props.sub: Beyond answering, four kinds of work.

beats.narration_text: Exploration, cleaning, visualization, and comparison. Not every question needs all four — but together they're what turns a raw export into something you can trust and act on.
beats.show: Four labelled chips resolve onto the stage: Explore · Clean · Visualize · Compare.

beats.narration_text: Exploration first. Point Claude at a mystery export — a CSV of column headers that don't quite make sense — and it describes the structure, flags outliers, and, most valuably, suggests the questions worth asking. Often the best question isn't the one you started with.
beats.show: Cryptic column headers flow in; a described structure, flagged outliers, and a list of suggested questions flow out.

beats.narration_text: Cleaning. Messy data is the norm, not the exception — inconsistent date formats, duplicate rows, impossible values like negative sales. The plugin does this tedious work, and reports exactly what it changed.
beats.show: A jumbled table settles into a tidy one as duplicates collapse and bad cells correct; a change-log accumulates alongside.

viz.before: messy rows: bad dates, duplicates, negatives

beats.narration_text: Data cleaning is the unglamorous prerequisite to any useful analysis — and it's the biggest reason people never start. Automate it, and the barrier to actually working with your numbers just falls away.
beats.show: A still of a cluttered desk of tangled receipts and printouts being squared into a neat stack; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: hands sorting a chaotic pile of paperwork into an ordered stack; documentary duotone, no readable text.

beats.narration_text: Visualization. Show me monthly revenue for the past year, and you get a chart — for a deck, for a partner, or just to feel your own trajectory. Line charts for trends, bars for comparisons, and you can always ask for something different.
beats.show: A single column of numbers lifts and morphs into a line chart, then swaps to a bar chart on cue.

props.label: Numbers become a picture.
props.caption: Visualization. Show me monthly revenue for the past year, and you get a chart — for a deck, for a partner, or just to fe

beats.narration_text: And comparison. How does this quarter stack against last? Is my acquisition cost climbing or falling? This is where analysis earns its keep — and where most people quit, because building the comparison by hand is tedious. Totals tell you where you are; comparisons tell you where you're heading.
beats.show: Two period bars sit side by side, then the delta between them animates into a rising or falling trend arrow.

beats.narration_text: Now make it a ritual.
beats.show: Segment card: 'The Monthly Habit' in Title Case serif on cream.

beats.narration_text: Every business has a question it's been avoiding. Start there. Point Claude at your invoices and your expenses and just ask: am I profitable, month to month? It reads both files, correlates them, and gives you a real read on your financial health.
beats.show: Onda code-block: the avoided question typed as a multi-line prompt against two named files, invoices and expenses.

props.code:
> here are my invoices and my expenses for the year.
  am I profitable month to month? which months were
  strongest and weakest? any expense category growing
  faster than revenue?

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
> here are my invoices and my expenses for the year.
  am I profitable month to month? which months were
  strongest and weakest? any expense category growing
  faster than revenue?

beats.narration_text: You'll know more about your financial position after this five-minute conversation than you did after a year of 'I should really look at the numbers.'
beats.show: A still of a person leaning into a laptop in the evening, focused, mid-conversation; slow push toward them.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: solo operator absorbed at a laptop in warm evening light; documentary duotone. Continuity start of vox_run R1.

beats.narration_text: So make it a ritual. First of the month, ten minutes: point Claude at last month's revenue and expenses and ask for a health check — total revenue, total expenses, margin, how it compares to the month before, and anything that looks off. It's the review a fractional CFO would give you, done in minutes.
beats.show: The camera pulls back from B15's operator to reveal a wall calendar with the first of the month circled; same room grammar as B15.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: same duotone treatment and room as B15, now wider to include a first-of-month calendar. Continuity end of vox_run R1.

beats.narration_text: Do it every month, and you're never surprised by your own numbers again. Each review stacks on the last until the trend is visible before it becomes a problem.
beats.show: Monthly check-in markers drop onto a timeline one by one and connect into a clear, readable trend line.

beats.narration_text: For service businesses, one question matters most: how concentrated is your revenue? Ask it directly — who are my top clients, what share rides on the top three, and is anyone quietly spending less than they used to?
beats.show: Onda code-block: the client-concentration prompt typed line by line into the composer.

props.code:
> analyze my client data. who are my top ten by revenue?
  how concentrated is it — what share comes from my top
  three? are any long-term clients spending less than
  they used to?

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
> analyze my client data. who are my top ten by revenue?
  how concentrated is it — what share comes from my top
  three? are any long-term clients spending less than
  they used to?

beats.narration_text: Concentration is one of the most important things a solo operator can know. If a single client is, say, forty percent of your income and you don't know it, you can't plan for the day they leave. The plugin makes that check trivial.
beats.show: Revenue splits into client slices; one slice grows and crosses a marked risk threshold, flagged in terracotta.

viz.after: one slice crosses the concentration-risk line

beats.narration_text: And the follow-up is cheap. Of those top clients, which are declining? What changed last quarter that might explain it? Follow the thread — the plugin makes each next question almost free, so ask it.
beats.show: A still of a finger tracing a downward line on a screen; slow drift along the line's descent.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a hand tracing a declining line on a monitor; documentary duotone, no readable figures.

beats.narration_text: Now the honest part.
beats.show: Segment card: 'You Own The Judgment' in Title Case serif on cream.

beats.narration_text: Here's the honest part. The plugin's analysis is only as trustworthy as two things: the data you fed it, and the assumptions underneath it. Clean inputs and sound assumptions give you a reliable read. Bad inputs give you confident, well-formatted nonsense.
beats.show: A PredictCard weighs two inputs — data quality and assumptions — feeding a single trust output that swings with them.

props.question: A PredictCard weighs two inputs — data quality and assumptions — feeding a single trust output that swings with them.

beats.narration_text: That's exactly why it reports what it cleaned — so you can verify the fixes before you trust the numbers built on top of them. The change-log isn't paperwork. It's the audit trail.
beats.show: The cleaning change-log presents line by line; each line gets a human check before the analysis above it unlocks.

beats.narration_text: The plugin can tell you a client is declining. It can't tell you whether to call them, discount, or let them go. It surfaces the number; you own the decision.
beats.show: A still of a person paused over a chart at a decision point; a terracotta ring draws on around 'the decision' as the words land.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person pausing thoughtfully over a printed chart; ring annotation added at previz.

beats.narration_text: Four habits make it sing. Ask a specific question, not 'analyze my data.' Iterate — your first question breeds a better one. Compare, don't just total. And export on a schedule, because the plugin can only work with the data you actually give it.
beats.show: Four habit tiles stack in order: Ask specific → Iterate → Compare → Export regularly.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The data plugin opens the spreadsheet you've been avoiding. It explores it, does the unglamorous cleaning, and answers real questions — which clients are declining, where you're actually profitable — in plain English. It's a monthly habit that keeps you from being surprised by your own numbers. And it's only as trustworthy as your data and your assumptions, so you keep the judgment.

Comparisons, not just totals — see it coming
A monthly habit: never surprised again
Only as good as your data + assumptions — you own the call

props.command: I have a spreadsheet of my invoices and a spreadsheet of my expenses for the past year. First, tell me what's in each file and flag anything that looks messy or wrong. Then give me a business-health check: am I profitable month to month, which months were strongest and weakest, and is any expense category growing faster than revenue? Then tell me the one follow-up question I should ask next.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: here's a spreadsheet of my invoices and one of my expenses for the year — tell me what's in each file and flag anything messy, then give me a business-health check: am I profitable month to month, which months were strongest and weakest, is any expense growing faster than revenue — and end with the one follow-up question I should ask next. It turns this chapter into a five-minute conversation about your actual money — run it, and notice the question it hands back. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
beats.show: Claude composer: the full data-review prompt typed in, ready to send.

props.command: I have a spreadsheet of my invoices and a spreadsheet of my expenses for the past year. First, tell me what's in each file and flag anything that looks messy or wrong. Then give me a business-health check: am I profitable month to month, which months were strongest and weakest, and is any expense category growing faster than revenue? Then tell me the one follow-up question I should ask next.

beats.narration_text: Claude, By the Numbers.
beats.show: Title outro card: 'Claude, By the Numbers' restated in serif over cream, handle and subline beneath.
